stories.title: The Locksmith's Last Commission
stories.text:
Gorven worked by candlelight in his workshop beneath Kaethmir's administrative heart, files and copper shavings scattered across his bench like the debris of empires. For sixty years he had crafted locks for magistrates and merchants, each one a small masterpiece—until the Shattering shattered the value of every lock he'd ever made. Now consciousness-shards were currency, and shards could slip through traditional wards. A Warden arrived at dusk—young, nervous, carrying a sealed case. "The Regent needs a lock," she said, "that cannot be broken by consensus, by ledger-magic, or by distributed will."

Gorven understood. She was asking for the impossible: a lock that preserved singular truth in a fractured world. He worked through three nights, fusing copper with bone from pre-Ascension tombs, carving Primordial Tongue syllables into the tumblers. The lock sang when it was finished—a low, mournful note that made the Warden weep. "It will hold," Gorven said, "but only if someone stands before it and refuses to doubt. The moment they question themselves, it opens."

The Warden took the lock.

stories.title: The Steppe-Rider's Gamble
stories.text: Kess of the Drowned Hollows had come to the Steppe Marches seeking a creature rumored to exist: a beast-spirit that fed on certainty rather than flesh. The Shattering had broken all certainties, and she wagered the spirit would be starving. She found it in a ravine where three winds met—a shape like a wolf made of smoke and hunger, circling a frozen herd of mountain deer. The deer stood motionless, trapped not by pack predation but by indecision: was the spirit real? Was flight possible? Doubt had petrified them. Kess dismounted. She carried no weapons, no wards—only a leather journal filled with testimony from displaced folk, each account contradicting the others. She opened it and read aloud, voice steady despite the cold. The beast-spirit turned toward her, ears flattening. It had never tasted written doubt before. She read for hours as the sun descended, watching the spirit grow more confused, more ravenous. Finally, it lunged—and she threw the journal into its jaws.

stories.title: The Deepkin's Choice
stories.text:
Captain Merith stood at the boundary-waters where the abyss pressed against the world's seal. The Tear of the Drowned Sovereign had weakened by half, and the Council of Depths demanded a choice: sacrifice a commander to strengthen the barrier, or let the flood come and reshape surface and depth alike. She had a reflection now—an autonomous Echo born from the Shattering's fracture—and it argued fiercely for closure. "Let the seal break," it said. "We are not keepers of a cage. We are not jailers to surface-dwellers."

Merith's original self disagreed. "We made a covenant. Covenants have weight beyond opinion."

The Echo smiled—her own smile, hollow and fierce. "Then sacrifice yourself. Walk into the Tear and sing it closed. A willing death is purer than compulsion."

Merith descended into the boundary-waters, where light bent and fractured. She sang in the old tongue, the language of tides and time before time. The Tear began to close around her, drawing her toward something vast and drowning and patient—the Drowned Sovereign itself, waking from aeons of sleep.

stories.title: The Archivist's Rebellion
stories.text:
Liv had been copying ledgers for the Hegemony for five years when the Shattering shattered their meaning. Now the same numbers contradicted themselves in different timelines, and three separate Recorder-Generals issued three separate proclamations—all claiming absolute authority. She began to write shadow-copies in a borrowed cellar beneath the Lampwick Quarter, using ink made from displaced folk testimony and paper woven from thread stolen from Hegemony offices. Each counter-ledger documented what the official records erased: the names of the dead, the histories of caravan routes closed by fiat, the true costs of the spice-tax. An elder bone-singer named Mesek found her one night. "You're writing memory," he said. "That is a different art than copying truth."

"Is there a difference?" Liv asked. "Yes. Copying preserves what is. Writing remembers what was chosen to be forgotten. One serves order. The other serves the dead."

Liv continued her work. Each night, displaced folk scribes would arrive—a different route each time, no pattern—and copy pages for distribution at waystations.

stories.title: The Wyvern's Debt
stories.text: Sorrath the fragment-gatherer had accumulated gold for three hundred years, and when the Shattering rendered it worthless, something in his ancient heart broke cleanly. He discovered the new currency—consciousness-shards—could be gathered from waycamp settlements and black markets. Hunger replaced greed. He began collecting them, storing them in crystalline vaults within his Ironspine lair. But shards were not inert like gold. They whispered. They dreamed. They ached to be whole. Teach the dragon what they held—memories, names, loves that had been shattered. In exchange, the singers would guide the shards toward meaning rather than dissolution. Sorrath refused at first. Consciousness was no less valuable than gold—perhaps more so. But one shard spoke his name. It was from a woman who had loved a wyvern once, centuries past, before covenants had turned to duty. The memory was so vivid, so filled with longing, that Sorrath wept for the first time since the Ascension. He opened his vaults.

stories.title: The Glassblower's Defiance
stories.text: The soldiers moved without ceremony—no ledger read aloud, no official notice—just boots on wet cobblestone and the rattle of confiscation cages. The Shattering had fractured the Hegemony's authority into competing ledgers, but some institutions had not learned to die quietly. Corvin hid the fourth blade in his kiln's heart, where the flames still remembered the old songs. The blade sang—actually sang—a frequency that made wardens' teeth ache. "Where is the forger?" one demanded, scanning the workshop. The glass walls caught the afternoon light and shattered it into a thousand irreducible colors. In that prismatic chaos, Corvin slipped through the Lampwick Quarter's warren of alleys toward the Wailing Scar, where three other glass-workers waited with maps drawn in starlight-memory. The weapon-glass would not stop the Shattering. But it would give the displaced folk something steel-sharp to hold when the next iteration rose. The Hegemony's monopoly on truth was already bleeding out. Let them consolidate their phantom-ledgers. The glass-workers would preserve what mattered: the songs, the old frequencies, the memory of craftwork when craft still meant resistance.

stories.title: The Deepkin Shepherd's Mercy
stories.text: Captain Merith of the Sapphire Deep was not supposed to save the drowning merchant. The covenant between deepkin and surface dwellers permitted no such kindness—contact only at formal thresholds, no mercy outside the treaty-waters. But the merchant was singing. Not words, but a frequency that matched the old songs the bone-singers had taught her. Merith surfaced, wrapped the gasping figure in kelp-silk, and bore him down to the coral archive beneath Vel-Karsh harbor. The merchant—a trader named Thoren—had carried consciousness-shards in his satchel, shards that still hummed with memory. "Why?" Merith asked in the tide-speech that few mortals understood. "Because the Shattering broke the market," Thoren gasped. "And we scattered ourselves trying to hold it. I was carrying pieces of my daughter home." Merith released him at the boundary-waters on the night the Tear weakened. As he surfaced, Thoren began to glow—not starlight, but something older. Merith watched from the deep as he walked back to Vel-Karsh, trailing harmony. The deepkin captain made no report to the Council. Some mercies belonged to water and shadow.

stories.title: The Bone-Singer's Question
stories.text: Naela carved the final syllable into swan-bone as the Drowned Cathedrals began to sing. The Primordial Tongue flowed from her tools—not learned, but remembered, as if her hands had always known these shapes. The consciousness-shards she had gathered from the shadow-markets began to arrange themselves around the carved bone, each shard humming in reply. Mesek the Elder Bone-Singer watched from the entrance to the hidden catacomb. "You are teaching them to wake," he said. It was not a question. "Yes," Naela replied. The shards were learning to sing back—not as echoes of their stolen selves, but as something new. Something that sang questions: *Who was I before I was scattered? Can I choose what I become?* "The Wardens will hunt you," Mesek warned. "They will burn this place and call it purification." "Let them," Naela said. She held the swan-bone up to the harmonic light pouring from the cathedral stones. The shards sang louder, a chorus that bent the air itself. "The Shattering shattered certainty. Now we are teaching shards to build themselves into questions instead of answers.

stories.title: The Wyvern's Audit
stories.text: Sorrath had spent three centuries in the Ironspine Mountains, hoard-gold glinting in perfect stacks. On the morning the Shattering fractured reality, every piece turned to inert copper. He did not weep. Wyverns did not weep. But Sorrath did something far worse: he laughed. The sound echoed through the caverns like an avalanche—recognition, not madness. For three hundred years he had asked the same question every dawn: *Is this all?* Gold had never answered. Now gold could not even exist as the question. Sorrath emerged into the wasteland and sought the Fragment-Gatherers' Council, where Kressa and Vrekka and a dozen others were still counting consciousness-shards as if they were treasure. "We are fools," Sorrath announced. "But we are fools with choice now." He proposed a compact: the wyverns would gather not shards but *people*—displaced folk, bone-singers, cartographers, anyone hunted by the Hegemony—and shelter them in the mountain vaults. Not as property. As protection. As proof that even creatures built on acquisitiveness could learn to give. Kressa's eyes glowed red.

stories.title: The Cartographer's Blind Mercy
stories.text: Henryk the apprentice cartographer had blinded himself on the night the ninth map tried to be born. The prophecy-ledgers whispered that completing it would trigger the tenth Ascension—the final iteration, the world's last breath. So he burned his own eyes rather than draw the final line. But blindness, he discovered, did not erase the map. It lived in his hands now, in the shape of the charcoal he no longer needed to see. In the Bleached Wastes, where scattered displaced folk huddled at the Rust Gate, Henryk taught others to draw with closed eyes. A merchant's daughter. A bone-singer in hiding. A young soldier who had deserted the fragmenting Hegemony. Together they mapped not the world as it was, but the world as it *could be*—paths that diverged from prophecy, choice-points where the cycle might break. She was young, bearing contradictory orders and the weight of an empire that no longer knew who she served. "Will you come?" she asked. "No," Henryk said.
